exercises.title: Two losses, one transformer
exercises.prompt: Transfusion keeps images continuous (no VQ-VAE) and trains one transformer with two losses: next-token prediction on text, diffusion/flow-matching on image patches. Explain why this escapes the quantization bottleneck of Chameleon/Emu3 and what it trades for that.
exercises.hint: Discrete VQ tokens cap image quality (rounding to a codebook). Transfusion keeps images as continuous patches and uses a diffusion loss on them → no quantization loss → diffusion-grade quality. Trade: two objectives + a hybrid attention mask, more complex than one NTP loss.
exercises.solution:
**The setup:** Chameleon and Emu3 (Lessons 11-12) discretize images into **VQ tokens** and use a single next-token loss. Transfusion takes the **opposite bet**: **drop the VQ-VAE entirely, keep image patches continuous**, and train **one transformer with two losses on the same weights**:
- **Text tokens → next-token prediction** (standard autoregressive cross-entropy).
- **Image patches → diffusion / flow-matching loss** (predict the noise/velocity on continuous patches, Phase 8.6/8.13).
Both objectives optimize the **same transformer backbone**.

**Why it escapes the quantization bottleneck:** the discrete approaches **round each continuous image patch to the nearest of K codebook entries** — a **lossy** step that caps reconstruction quality below continuous-space methods (Lesson 12's ceiling). Transfusion **never quantizes images** — patches stay **continuous** and are modeled with a **diffusion loss** (which operates natively in continuous space, like Stable Diffusion). So there's **no codebook to round to, no quantization loss** → the image quality ceiling rises to **diffusion-grade**, escaping the discrete bottleneck. You get the best of both: **one unified transformer** (like Chameleon) but **continuous-space image quality** (like diffusion).

**What it trades for that:**
- **Two objectives instead of one:** you must balance an NTP loss and a diffusion loss on shared weights — more complex training than Emu3's single clean next-token loss.
- **A hybrid attention mask:** text needs **causal** attention (autoregressive), image patches need **bidirectional** attention (diffusion denoises all patches jointly) — so one transformer must apply **different masks to different token types** (next exercise). More architectural complexity.
- **More code complexity:** a diffusion sampling loop for images alongside autoregressive text decoding.
So Transfusion trades **simplicity (one loss)** for **quality (continuous, no quantization ceiling)**. Its architecture is a close cousin of **MMDiT (Stable Diffusion 3)**, and it represents the **'continuous + dual-loss'** corner of the unified-model design space — the answer to 'how do we keep Chameleon/Emu3's unification but get diffusion-quality images?' The answer: don't quantize, run two losses on one backbone.

exercises.title: The hybrid attention mask and two-loss training
exercises.prompt: Implement a two-loss trainer where text tokens get NTP loss and image patches get diffusion MSE loss on one backbone. Explain why bidirectional attention across image patches plus causal attention over text tokens is the right mask choice, and how the two losses combine.
exercises.hint: Mask: text tokens attend causally (left-to-right); image patches attend bidirectionally (all patches see each other, like an image is a whole); text can attend to image, image attends within itself + preceding text. Loss = NTP(text) + diffusion_mse(image patches). Causal for generation order, bidirectional because an image isn't sequential.
exercises.solution:
```python
import numpy as np

def hybrid_mask(token_types):
    """token_types: list of 'text' or 'image'. Build the attention mask."""
    n = len(token_types)
    mask = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=bool)
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            if token_types[i] == 'text':
                mask[i, j] = (j <= i)              # text: causal (only past)
            else:  # image patch
                same_image = in_same_image(i, j)  # bidirectional within its image
                mask[i, j] = same_image or (token_types[j]=='text' and j < image_start(i))
    return mask

def transfusion_loss(model, batch):
    out = model(batch.tokens, mask=hybrid_mask(batch.types))
    text_loss = cross_entropy(out[batch.text_pos], batch.text_targets)   # NTP
    # image patches: predict the noise added (diffusion / flow-matching)
    img_loss = np.mean((out[batch.img_pos] - batch.noise_target) ** 2)   # diffusion MSE
    return text_loss + img_loss          # both optimize the same backbone
```

**Why this mask (causal text + bidirectional image):**
- **Text → causal attention:** text is generated **autoregressively, left to right** — each token must depend **only on previous tokens** (next-token prediction requires not seeing the future, Phase 7.7). So text tokens attend causally.
- **Image patches → bidirectional attention:** an **image is not sequential** — all its patches exist 'at once' and are mutually dependent (the top-left depends on the bottom-right and vice versa; it's a 2D whole). **Diffusion denoises all patches jointly** (the denoiser sees the whole noisy image each step), so image patches must attend **bidirectionally** — every patch sees every other patch in the same image. Forcing causal attention on image patches would cripple generation (a patch couldn't use the full spatial context). This is exactly why diffusion models use bidirectional/full attention over the image (Phase 8.7's DiT).
- **Cross-modal:** text can attend to **preceding** images (to condition on them, like reading), and image patches attend to the **text prompt before them** (to condition generation on the prompt) plus **all patches within their own image**.
So the mask is **causal for the sequential modality (text) and bidirectional for the spatial modality (image)** — matching each modality's true structure. One transformer, two attention regimes, selected by token type.

**How the two losses combine:** each token type contributes its **appropriate loss on the same forward pass / same weights**: text positions get **next-token cross-entropy**, image positions get the **diffusion MSE** (predict the noise/velocity on the continuous patches). The total loss is their **sum** (possibly weighted) — `L = L_text + λ·L_image` — and both **backpropagate through the shared backbone**, so the transformer learns to do *both jobs* with one set of weights. At inference, you **generate text autoregressively** (causal, sample tokens) and **generate images by running the diffusion sampling loop** over the image positions (bidirectional, denoise over steps) — the same model, two decoding procedures. This is the core trick: **one set of weights, two modality-appropriate losses, two modality-appropriate attention masks** — unifying autoregressive text and diffusion images in a single transformer.

tests.desc: a text token attends to itself

exercises.title: Transfusion vs Chameleon vs MMDiT, and the unified-model verdict
exercises.prompt: Compare Transfusion-style (continuous images, diffusion loss) to Chameleon-style (discrete images, NTP) on compute, quality, and code complexity, name MMDiT's contribution (modality-specific weights + joint attention), and give the practical verdict on which unified-model approach to choose for a given goal.
exercises.hint: Transfusion: higher image quality (continuous), but two losses + diffusion sampling complexity. Chameleon: simpler (one loss, one decode), but quantization ceiling. MMDiT: per-modality weights at each block, joint attention at the residual stream (SD3). Verdict: quality-critical gen → continuous/Transfusion/MMDiT; simplicity/unified-AR → discrete.
exercises.solution:
**Transfusion (continuous, diffusion loss) vs Chameleon (discrete, NTP):**
| | **Transfusion-style** | **Chameleon-style** |
|---|---|---|
| **Image quality** | **higher** — continuous, no quantization ceiling (diffusion-grade) | **capped** by VQ quantization (codebook rounding) |
| **Compute / decode** | image gen needs a **diffusion sampling loop** (multiple denoising steps over patches) | image gen is **autoregressive** (sequential token-by-token); both have costs |
| **Training** | **two losses** (NTP + diffusion) + **hybrid mask** — more to balance | **one clean loss** (NTP), one mask regime — simpler |
| **Code complexity** | higher (two objectives, diffusion sampler, dual attention) | lower (uniform next-token machinery) |
| **Unification** | text + continuous images in one transformer | text + discrete images/video in one transformer |

The core tradeoff: **Chameleon/Emu3 = simpler (one loss, uniform decode) but quantization-ceiling'd; Transfusion = higher quality (continuous) but more complex (two losses, two attention regimes, diffusion sampling).**

**MMDiT's contribution (Stable Diffusion 3's architecture, a Transfusion cousin):** MMDiT (Multimodal Diffusion Transformer) refines the 'one transformer, two modalities' idea with two key choices:
1. **Modality-specific weights at each block:** text tokens and image tokens get **separate projection weights** (separate Q/K/V, separate MLPs) within each transformer block — recognizing that text and image have **different statistics and need different processing** — rather than forcing identical weights on both (which contributes to Chameleon's instability, Lesson 11).
2. **Joint attention at the residual stream:** despite separate per-modality weights, the modalities **attend to each other jointly** (image and text tokens are concatenated and attend together in the attention operation) at the shared residual stream — so they **fuse and condition on each other** while still being processed by modality-specific weights elsewhere.
This 'separate weights, joint attention' design gives **better text-image binding and stability** than fully-shared weights, and powers SD3/Flux (Phase 8.7). It's the production refinement of the Transfusion concept.

**Practical verdict — which unified approach to choose:**
- **Quality-critical image/video generation → continuous (Transfusion / MMDiT / diffusion).** If the **image/video output quality is paramount** (production image generation, creative tools), use **continuous representations with a diffusion loss** — you can't accept the quantization ceiling. MMDiT-style (separate weights + joint attention) is the proven production choice.
- **Maximal simplicity / unified AR across many modalities → discrete (Emu3 / Chameleon).** If you want **one clean objective, one decode procedure, and easy extension to many modalities** (text, image, video, audio all as tokens), and the **quantization ceiling is acceptable**, the **discrete next-token approach** is simpler to build, scale, and reason about — and Emu3 proved it's competitive.
- **Fast parallel image decode → masked discrete diffusion (Show-o, Lesson 14)** — a middle path (discrete but parallel few-step decode).
- **Best of understanding AND generation without compromising either → decoupled encoders (Janus-Pro, Lesson 15).**
**The meta-point:** there's **no single winner** in the unified-model space — it's a **tradeoff map** between **image quality (continuous > discrete)**, **simplicity (single-loss discrete > dual-loss continuous)**, **decode speed (parallel diffusion/masked > sequential AR)**, and **understanding-vs-generation balance**. Transfusion/MMDiT occupy the **'continuous, high-quality, dual-loss'** corner — the right choice when generation quality matters most — while Emu3/Chameleon occupy the **'discrete, simple, single-loss'** corner — the right choice for unification and simplicity. Knowing *what each corner buys and costs* (quality ceiling, loss complexity, decode procedure, modality reach) is how you pick — the same 'match the architecture to the goal' discipline that runs through the whole phase.
